вЂњWe often learn React by building components. But understanding why React works the way it does changes how you design applications.вЂќ
Introduction
With AI transforming software development, itвЂ™s never been easier to generate React code.
AI can scaffold components, write hooks, fix bugs, and even build entire applications.
But one thing IвЂ™ve been reminding myself lately is this:
AI can help us write code faster, but understanding the fundamentals helps us build better software.
So I decided to start a series called Deep Concepts, where I explore the engineering decisions behind the technologies we use every day.
The first topic had to be React Fiber.
If youвЂ™ve ever wondered why features like startTransition, Suspense, or Concurrent Rendering exist, the answer starts with Fiber.
Before Fiber: How React Originally Worked
When React was first released, its rendering engine was built on what is now known as the Stack Reconciler.
Whenever your application state changed, React would start from the root component and recursively visit every component that needed updating.
Imagine this component tree:
<App>
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Navbar
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Sidebar
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Dashboard
в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Chart
в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Orders
в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ News
в””в”Ђв”Ђ FooterWhen Dashboard changed, React would walk through the tree and perform all necessary calculations before updating the DOM.
The important limitation was:
Rendering was synchronous.
Once React started rendering, it couldnвЂ™t pause.
It couldnвЂ™t answer user input.
It couldnвЂ™t prioritise urgent work.
It had to finish everything before giving control back to the browser.
For small applications this worked well.
For applications with thousands of components, it became a serious problem.
Why Was This a Problem?
Browsers try to render at around 60 frames per second.
That gives them roughly 16 milliseconds to complete each frame.
Within those 16 milliseconds the browser must:

вЂў Execute JavaScript

вЂў Calculate styles

вЂў Perform layout

вЂў Paint pixels

вЂў Respond to user input
If JavaScript blocks the main thread for too long, users notice:

вЂў scrolling becomes janky

вЂў typing lags

вЂў animations stutter

вЂў buttons feel unresponsive
ReactвЂ™s rendering process was becoming that bottleneck.
The React team realised something fundamental:
Rendering doesnвЂ™t always need to happen immediately.
Some updates are urgent.
Others can wait a few milliseconds.
But the existing architecture couldnвЂ™t make that distinction.
The Birth of React Fiber
Rather than patching the existing renderer, the React team вЂ” including major contributors such as Andrew Clark and other engineers at Meta вЂ” rebuilt ReactвЂ™s reconciliation engine from the ground up.
The result was React Fiber, introduced in React 16.
Fiber wasnвЂ™t a new API.
It wasnвЂ™t a new rendering library.
It was an entirely new internal architecture.
Its primary goal was simple:
Make rendering interruptible.
What Exactly Is a Fiber?
A Fiber is a JavaScript object that represents one React component and the work associated with it.
Instead of processing an entire tree in one go, React breaks rendering into many small units of work.
Each component has its own Fiber.
Conceptually, a Fiber contains information such as:
Fiber
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ type (Component)
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ key
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ pendingProps
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ memoizedProps
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ memoizedState
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ child
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ sibling
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ return (parent)
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ alternate
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ flags
в””в”Ђв”Ђ lanesYou donвЂ™t interact with these fields directly, but they allow React to keep track of everything it needs to know about a component during rendering.
How Fiber Represents Your Component Tree
Suppose you write:
<App>
<Header />
<Dashboard>
<Chart />
<Orders />
</Dashboard>
</App>Internally React builds a linked structure that looks conceptually like this:
App
в”‚
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ child в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв–є Header
в”‚ в”‚
в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ sibling в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв–є Dashboard
в”‚ в”‚
в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ child в”Ђв–є Chart
в”‚ в”‚
в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ sibling в”Ђв–є OrdersUnlike a normal tree stored in nested arrays, this linked structure lets React efficiently move between parents, children, and siblings while doing work.
Current Tree vs Work-In-Progress Tree
One of FiberвЂ™s most elegant ideas is double buffering.
React maintains two versions of your UI.
Current Tree
The tree currently visible to users.
Current TreeApp
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Header
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Dashboard
в””в”Ђв”Ђ FooterWork-In-Progress Tree (WIP)
When state changes, React doesnвЂ™t immediately modify the current tree.
Instead it creates a second version.
Work In ProgressApp
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Header
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Dashboard*
в””в”Ђв”Ђ FooterReact performs all calculations on this Work-In-Progress tree.
Only when everything is complete does React swap the trees.
Users never see a partially rendered UI.
Why Is This Better?
Imagine editing a Word document.
Instead of changing the original while people are reading it, you make edits on a copy.
Once everything is correct, you replace the original.
ThatвЂ™s exactly how Fiber works.
This approach prevents inconsistent UI and enables interruptible rendering.
The Render Phase
During rendering React asks:

вЂў Which components changed?

вЂў Should they re-render?

вЂў What new UI should exist?
This phase creates or updates the Work-In-Progress tree.
Importantly:
No DOM updates happen yet.
The render phase is pure computation.
Because of Fiber, React can pause this work if something more important happens.
The Commit Phase
Once rendering finishes, React enters the Commit Phase.
Now React performs real work:

вЂў Update the DOM

вЂў Attach refs

вЂў Run layout effects

вЂў Run passive effects
Unlike rendering, the commit phase cannot be interrupted because the DOM must remain consistent.
This separation вЂ” compute first, commit later вЂ” is one of the biggest architectural improvements introduced by Fiber.
Scheduling: Not Every Update Is Equal
Consider these two updates:

вЂў A user types into a search box.

вЂў A background analytics chart refreshes.
Should both happen with the same urgency?
Probably not.
Fiber introduced the idea that updates have different priorities.
Urgent user interactions should happen first.
Background work can wait.
This is what allows React to feel responsive even when large parts of the application are updating.
A Quick Note on Lanes
Modern React represents update priorities using Lanes.
Think of a lane as a category of work.
Conceptually:
Sync Lane
в†‘
Immediate user interactionsDefault Lane
в†‘
Normal renderingTransition Lane
в†‘
UI updates that can waitIdle Lane
в†‘
Background workWhen you call startTransition(), React marks those updates as less urgent, allowing more important interactionsвЂ”like typing or clickingвЂ”to stay responsive.
You donвЂ™t need to manage lanes yourself, but understanding that they exist helps explain why React behaves the way it does.
What Fiber Made Possible
Fiber became the foundation for many modern React features:

вЂў Concurrent Rendering

вЂў Suspense

вЂў startTransition

вЂў useDeferredValue

вЂў Streaming server rendering

вЂў Better scheduling

вЂў Improved responsiveness under load
Most developers use these APIs every day without realizing they all rely on the architectural decisions made by Fiber.
Lessons Beyond React
The most interesting thing about Fiber isnвЂ™t that React became вЂњfaster.вЂќ
ItвЂ™s that React became interruptible.
The public API changed only gradually.
Under the hood, however, the architecture was completely redesigned to support the next decade of web applications.
ThatвЂ™s a valuable engineering lesson:
Great software often evolves not by adding more features, but by strengthening the foundations so future innovation becomes possible.
Final Thoughts
Learning React hooks and APIs is essential.
Understanding why React was redesigned helps you reason about performance, rendering, state updates, and user experience at a much deeper level.
As AI continues to accelerate software development, I believe engineers who understand the systems beneath the abstractions will continue to build the most reliable, scalable, and thoughtful applications.
This is the first article in my Deep Concepts series.
In the next article, weвЂ™ll go deeper into the internals of a Fiber node вЂ” exploring fields like alternate, memoizedState, updateQueue, flags, and how React traverses the Fiber tree during reconciliation.
Thanks for reading, and if you found this useful, IвЂ™d love to hear what React internals youвЂ™d like to explore next.
